analogy: Think of "Indian Economy 1950-1990" through the chapter's own opening images: After studying this chapter, the learners will • come to know the goals of India’s five year plans • know about the development policies in different sectors such as agriculture and industry from 1950-1990 • learn to think about the merits and limitations of a... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: INDIAN ECONOMY, 17 INDIAN ECONOMY 1950-1990, 2.1 INTRODUCTION, 18 INDIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Indian Economy 1950-1990 is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that after studying this chapter, the learners will • come to know the goals of India’s five year plans • know about the development policies in different sectors such as agriculture and industry from 1950-1990 • learn to think about the merits and limitations of a regulated economy.

The text explains that 17 INDIAN ECONOMY 1950-1990 2.1 INTRODUCTION On 15 August 1947, India woke to a new dawn of freedom.

The text explains that finally we were masters of our own destiny after some two hundred years of British rule; the job of nation building was now in our own hands.

The text explains that the leaders of independent India had to decide, among other things, the type of economic system most suitable for our nation, a system which would promote the welfare of all rather than a few.

The text explains that there are different types of economic systems (see Box 2.1) and among them, socialism appealed to Jawaharlal Nehru the most.

The text explains that however, he was not in favour of the kind of socialism established in the former Soviet Union where all the means of production, i.e. all the factories and farms in the country, were owned by the government.

The text explains that it is not possible in a d e m o c r a c y l i k e I n d i a f o r t h e government to change the ownership pattern of land and other properties of its citizens in the way that it was done in the former Soviet Union.

The text explains that nehru, and many other leaders and thinkers of the newly independent India, sought an alternative to the extreme versions of capitalism and socialism.

The text explains that basically sympathising with the socialist outlook, they found the answer in an economic system which, in their view, combined the best features of socialism without its drawbacks.

The text explains that in this view, India would be a socialist society with a strong public sector but also with private property and democracy; the government would plan (see Box 2.2) for the The central objective of Planning in India... is to initiate a process of development which will raise the living standards and open out to the people new opportunities for a richer and more varied life.

The text explains that first Five Year Plan Work These Out ÿ Prepare a chart on the different types of economic systems prevalent in the world.

The text explains that list out the countries as capitalist, socialist and mixed economy. ÿ Plan a class trip to an agriculture farm.

The text explains that divide the class into seven groups with each group to plan a specific goal, for example, the purpose of the visit, money expenditure involved, time taken, resources, people accompanying the group and who need to be contacted, possible places of visit, possible questions to be asked etc.

The text explains that now, with the help of your teacher, compile these specific goals and compare with long-term goals of successful visit to an agricultural farm.

The text explains that 18 INDIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT Box 2.1: Types of Economic Systems Every society has to answer three questions ÿWhat goods and services should be produced in the country? ÿHow should the goods and services be produced?

The text explains that should producers use more human labour or more capital (machines) for producing things? ÿHow should the goods and services be distributed among people?

The text explains that one answer to these questions is to depend on the market forces of supply and demand.

The text explains that in a market economy, also called capitalism, only those consumer goods will be produced that are in demand, i.e., goods that can be sold profitably either in the domestic or in the foreign markets.

The text explains that in a capitalist society the goods produced are distributed among people not on the basis of what people need but on the basis of Purchasing Power—the ability to buy goods and services.

The text explains that such a society did not appeal to Jawaharlal Nehru, our first prime minister, for it meant that the great majority of people of the country would be left behind without the chance to improve their quality of life.

The text explains that a socialist society answers the three questions in a totally different manner.

The text explains that in a socialist society the government decides what goods are to be produced in accordance with the needs of society.

The text explains that it is assumed that the government knows what is good for the people of the country and so the desires of individual consumers are not given much importance.

The text explains that the government decides how goods are to be produced and how they should be distributed.

The text explains that strictly, a socialist society has no private property since everything is owned by the state.

The text explains that most economies are mixed economies, i.e. the government and the market together answer the three questions of what to produce, how to produce and how to distribute what is produced.

The text explains that in a mixed economy, the market will provide whatever goods and services it can produce well, and the government will provide essential goods and services which the market fails to do.

The text explains that 19 INDIAN ECONOMY 1950-1990 economy with the private sector being encouraged to be part of the plan effort.

The text explains that the ‘Industrial Policy Resolution’ of 1948 and the Directive Principles of the Indian Constitution reflected this outlook.

The text explains that due to limited resources, a choice has to be made in each plan about which of the goals is to be given primary importance.

The text explains that a plan spells out how the resources of a nation should be put to use.

The text explains that in 1945 he was made a Fellow (member) of Britain’s Royal Society, one of the most prestigious organisations of scientists; only the most outstanding scientists are made members of this Society.

The text explains that source: Sukhamoy Chakravarty, ‘Mahalanobis, Prasanta Chandra’ in John Eatwell et.al, (Eds.) The New Palgrave Dictionary: Economic Development, W.W.

The text explains that 21 INDIAN ECONOMY 1950-1990 economics, is steady increase in the Gross Domestic Product (GDP).

The text explains that the GDP of a country is derived from the different sectors of the economy, namely the agricultural sector, the industrial sector and the service sector.

The text explains that the contribution made by each of these sectors makes up the structural composition of the economy.

The text explains that however, modernisation does not refer only to the use of new technology but also to changes in social outlook such as the recognition that women should have the same rights as men.

The text explains that in a traditional society, women are supposed to remain at home while men work.

The text explains that a modern society makes use of the talents of women in the work place — in banks, factories, schools etc. — and such a society in most occassions is also prosperous.

The text explains that self-reliance: A nation can promote economic growth and modernisation by using its own resources or by using resources imported from other nations.

The text explains that explain how import substitution can protect domestic industry.

The text explains that why and how was private sector regulated under the IPR 1956?

The text explains that seeds that give large proportion of output 2.

The text explains that quantity of goods that can be imported Product 3.

The text explains that the money value of all the final goods and services produced within the economy in one year 5.

The text explains that improvements in the field of agriculture to increase its productivity 6.

The text explains that t h e m o n e t a r y a s s i s t a n c e g i v e n b y go v e r n m e n t f o r p r o d u c t i o n a c t i v i t i e s .

The text explains that forty Years After Independence, in Bimal Jalan, (Ed.).

The text explains that industrial Policy and Controls, in Bimal Jalan (Ed.).

The text explains that agriculture: Policy and Performance, in Bimal Jalan, (Ed.).

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Economic Development, Chapter 2: Indian Economy 1950-1990

options: The ‘Industrial Policy Resolution’ of 1948 and the Directive Principles of the Indian Constitution reflected this outlook.

2.2 THE GOALS OF FIVE YEAR PLANS A plan should have some clearly specified goals.
The goals of the five year plans were: growth, modernisation, self-reliance and equity.
